planting.note: Plant bare-root or container stock in early spring or fall in well-drained soil; seeds require a complex double dormancy (18+ months of stratification) and are best started from nursery stock. Named cultivars like 'Paul's Scarlet' and 'Crimson Cloud' are propagated by grafting.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; English hawthorn is the traditional hedgerow hawthorn of Britain and Europe, historically used to create stockproof field boundaries and now cultivated primarily for its spectacular late-spring flower display. The popular double-flowered cultivar 'Paul's Scarlet' (Crimson Cloud in North America) produces vivid deep pink to red double flowers that smother the thorny branches before the leaves fully emerge. The small red fruits (haws) ripen in fall and are consumed by thrushes, waxwings, and numerous other birds. The thorny dense habit makes it one of the most effective wildlife-friendly hedging plants for gardens. More susceptible to leaf blight diseases than native American hawthorns in humid summer climates.

planting.note: Plant container or bare-root stock in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil; seeds require double dormancy of 18+ months and are best obtained from a specialist nursery. The plant typically begins fruiting in 4-6 years from transplant.

directions: Plant in moist to moderately dry, well-drained soil in full sun to light shade; black hawthorn is a widespread native of western North America from Alaska to California and east to the Rocky Mountains, producing distinctive shiny black fruits (rather than the red of most hawthorns) that ripen in late summer. The blackish-purple haws are sweet and flavorful, traditionally eaten fresh and dried by Pacific Northwest Native peoples including the Klallam, Nlaka'pamux, and many others. The thorny thickets provide exceptional nesting habitat for songbirds. White spring flowers attract numerous pollinators. The fruit is also made into jam, jelly, and wine. More tolerant of wet soils than most hawthorns and common along stream banks in its native range.
sources: University of British Columbia Botanical Garden

planting.note: Plant divisions or container stock in moist, humus-rich, acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; this fern is semi-evergreen to evergreen in milder climates, making it a valuable year-round groundcover in shaded gardens.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; common wood fern is among the most widely distributed native ferns in eastern North America and one of the most practical for garden use, being semi-evergreen (holding its green fronds into early winter in most climates), adaptable to both moist and moderately dry soils under forest canopy, and vigorous enough to compete with tree roots. The finely divided, lacy fronds have a fresh, bright green color and a distinctive triangular outline. Unlike the cinnamon or royal ferns, this species grows well in drier shade — under dense deciduous trees where the soil dries in summer — making it invaluable for difficult shaded spots. Provide adequate organic matter at planting and mulch annually with shredded leaves for best results.

planting.note: Hairy bittercress is primarily harvested as a foraged weed that appears in garden beds in early spring and fall; if growing intentionally, direct sow in late summer or early fall in moist, shaded, or partly shaded soil. Harvesting small plants before they flower keeps the bed clean while providing fresh greens.

directions: Harvest young plants from shaded garden beds and pathways in late winter through early spring when they appear as small rosettes; hairy bittercress is a widespread annual or overwintering weed of gardens, nurseries, and cultivated ground throughout North America that is almost universally underappreciated as food. The small, watercress-like leaves have a pleasant, sharp, peppery flavor exactly like watercress and can be eaten raw in salads, added to sandwiches, or used as a garnish — ideally harvested before the white flowers open when flavor is best. The spring-loaded seed capsules shatter explosively when ripe, scattering seeds widely; harvesting before flowering prevents this and gives you both the greens and a cleaner garden. An excellent early-spring foraged green requiring no cultivation whatsoever.

planting.note: Direct sow nuts in fall for spring germination after cold stratification; or cold-stratify nuts for 90-120 days in the refrigerator and sow in early spring. Turkish hazel is one of the best hazelnut trees for challenging urban conditions.

directions: Plant in well-drained, alkaline to neutral, even dry or compacted soil in full sun; Turkish hazel is the most drought-tolerant and urban-adaptable of all hazelnuts, renowned in the horticultural trade as one of the few large nut trees that tolerates dry, alkaline soils, compacted city conditions, and air pollution — a remarkable feat for any tree. It is used as a rootstock for grafting cultivated filbert and hazelnut varieties, and the straight species produces small edible nuts enclosed in deeply fringed husks. The distinctive corky, deeply ridged bark and broadly pyramidal form make it an ornamental specimen tree even when not in nut. Long-lived and very low-maintenance; used extensively in European forestry and urban planting programs.
